key_insight: Defect-free FeS2(100) terraces can host two distinct self-assembled L-cysteine supramolecular phases, showing that pristine mineral surfaces—not only defects—can drive prebiotic molecular organization.
engineering_relevance: The work highlights how atomically ordered mineral interfaces can be engineered or selected to control surface self-assembly and reaction pathways in catalyst and origin-of-life materials research.
method: Scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy (STM/STS) on defect-free pyrite terraces
Density functional theory (DFT) to explain intermolecular and substrate-driven interactions
Identification of trimer-based interactions underlying the observed patterns
evidence_signal: The abstract reports two experimentally observed supramolecular phases of L-cysteine on ordered FeS2 terraces, supported by STM/STS measurements and DFT analysis.
watch_item: The findings are specific to highly ordered, defect-free pyrite terraces, so behavior may change significantly on more realistic, defect-rich natural surfaces.
paper_title: Prebiotic Chemistry Assemblies of L-Cysteine on Defect-Free Pyrite Terraces
